## Abstract

This study investigates the relationship between county-level social vulnerability indicators and the distribution of disaster assistance using machine learning and spatial modeling techniques. Using the 2018 CDC/ATSDR Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) indicators as predictors and FEMA Individual Household Program (IHP) assistance from 2018-2020 as the target outcome, the project evaluates whether incorporating spatial structure improves predictive performance. A nonlinear multilayer perceptron (MLP) baseline and a graph convolutional network (GCN) are implemented within a unified regression framework to compare tabular and spatial learning approaches.

The analysis uses log-transformed socioeconomic indicators, standardized features, and fixed train/validation/test splits to ensure consistent evaluation. Hyperparameters for both models are selected through controlled ablation experiments, and the best-performing configurations are retrained before final evaluation on a held-out test set.

Results show that the MLP baseline outperforms the spatial GCN model across evaluation metrics. The best MLP configuration achieves a test RMSE of 2.0048 and an $R^2$ of 0.5712, while the GCN achieves a test RMSE of approximately 2.4050 and an $R^2$ of 0.3829. Overall, the results suggest that while social vulnerability indicators provide meaningful signals for predicting disaster assistance outcomes, additional spatial modeling does not necessarily yield improvements when the underlying spatial dependency in the target variable is limited.

The study provides a reproducible modeling framework for integrating vulnerability indicators, disaster assistance data, and graph-based learning methods, while highlighting the importance of strong tabular baselines when evaluating spatial machine learning approaches.

## Introduction

Social vulnerability reflects how community-level social and demographic conditions influence the ability of populations to prepare for, withstand, and recover from hazards. In disaster management and public health, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention / Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry Social Vulnerability Index (CDC/ATSDR SVI) has become an important tool for identifying areas where communities may face disproportionate risk due to factors such as socioeconomic disadvantage, household composition, minority status, housing conditions, and transportation access. The index was designed to support emergency planning and resource allocation by identifying communities that may require additional assistance before, during, and after disasters. 

Recent evaluations have shown that SVI performs particularly well in preparedness, response, and recovery contexts for moderate and severe hazards, although its predictive ability is more limited in sudden extreme events where physical exposure may dominate outcomes. In such cases, the severity and location of the hazard itself—such as the path of a hurricane, wildfire spread, flood inundation depth, or seismic intensity—can exert a stronger influence on impacts than underlying socioeconomic conditions. 

While SVI provides a structured way to characterize vulnerability, an important practical question remains: *to what extent do these vulnerability indicators correspond to the distribution of disaster assistance in practice?* In other words, if social vulnerability is intended to guide resource prioritization, counties with higher vulnerability would be expected to experience greater need for recovery support following disasters. Evaluating this relationship can help assess whether vulnerability indicators meaningfully align with observed disaster response outcomes and whether they can support forward-looking planning.

Motivated by this question, the present study examines whether county-level social vulnerability indicators can be used to predict disaster assistance outcomes following hazard events. Specifically, the analysis uses county-level indicators from the 2018 CDC/ATSDR Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) as predictors and disaster assistance data from the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) Individuals and Households Program aggregated across 2018–2020 disaster events. The selected time window captures several major disaster events across the United States, including hurricanes, wildfires, floods, and the early federal disaster response to the COVID-19 pandemic.

By linking vulnerability indicators measured in 2018 to disaster assistance outcomes during the 2018–2020 period, the study evaluates whether patterns of social vulnerability are associated with the distribution of disaster recovery support across U.S. counties. Counties are represented as nodes within a geographic graph in which neighboring regions are connected through spatial adjacency relationships, enabling the analysis to incorporate spatial context alongside socioeconomic indicators. Understanding these relationships has direct implications for policy and disaster preparedness. If vulnerability indicators are strongly associated with observed disaster outcomes and assistance patterns, they may provide useful signals for identifying communities likely to require additional support after disasters occur. Conversely, weak relationships may suggest that other factors—such as hazard exposure, environmental conditions, or infrastructure resilience—play a larger role in shaping recovery needs. By evaluating how well vulnerability indicators explain disaster assistance outcomes from 2018–2020, this study contributes to ongoing efforts to improve data-driven approaches to emergency planning and equitable resource allocation.

## Related Work

Foundational work on social vulnerability established that disaster impacts are shaped not only by physical exposure to hazards, but also by underlying social and economic conditions that influence a community’s capacity to prepare for, respond to, and recover from disasters. Since its introduction, SVI has become one of the most widely used operational tools for identifying socially vulnerable communities in the United States. More recently, a CDC/ATSDR validation assessment found that SVI performs favorably overall, particularly for response and recovery contexts associated with moderate and severe hazards. However, the assessment also noted weaker applicability for predicting mortality in sudden, extreme events where physical damage and hazard exposure may dominate outcomes.

Machine learning approaches have begun to appear in the social vulnerability literature, though the body of work remains relatively limited and heterogeneous. Zhao et al. explored the use of machine learning and expanded data sources for constructing social vulnerability indices across multiple countries, demonstrating that nonlinear models and richer datasets can reveal important vulnerability drivers such as wealth disparities. Similarly, Kalaycioglu et al. applied several supervised learning algorithms to predict household-level social vulnerability in Istanbul and found that artificial neural networks performed best among the evaluated models, suggesting that machine learning methods may better capture complex interactions among socioeconomic variables. Yokoyama and Takefuji further examined machine-learning models built using CDC SVI variables and emphasized that model interpretability should be evaluated relative to classical statistical associations rather than inferred solely from predictive performance. In a related public health application, Yedinak et al. incorporated CDC SVI indicators alongside other county-level predictors in a machine learning framework for neighborhood-level vulnerability assessment, illustrating how vulnerability-related measures can support applied risk stratification.

Despite these advances, an important gap remains in the subject. Existing machine learning studies on social vulnerability typically rely on tabular modeling approaches and do not explicitly incorporate spatial relationships between geographic units. Most prior work either predicts a pre-constructed vulnerability score, evaluates feature importance within machine learning models, or uses SVI variables as inputs to related downstream prediction tasks. However, disaster impacts and recovery needs often exhibit strong spatial patterns because neighboring regions frequently experience similar hazard exposures and share infrastructure, economic, and environmental conditions. 

To the best of current knowledge, there is limited work in applying graph neural networks (GNNs) to county-level vulnerability indicators in order to model spatial relationships when predicting disaster-related outcomes. This gap motivates the present study, which extends existing tabular machine learning approaches by introducing a spatially relational learning framework. By incorporating adjacency relationships between neighboring counties, graph-based models may provide a natural mechanism for capturing spatial dependence that may influence patterns of disaster impacts and recovery needs across regions.

## Project Objectives

To guide the scope of the study, the following objectives are defined to examine how county-level social vulnerability indicators relate to observed disaster outcomes and to evaluate whether incorporating spatial context improves predictive modeling:

1. Conduct exploratory data analysis (EDA) to examine the distribution, scale, and relationships among the Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) indicators to better understand the statistical and geographic structure of the dataset prior to model development.
2. Establish a deep learning baseline for tabular prediction by training a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) to model nonlinear relationships between the percentage-based vulnerability variables and county-level disaster assistance outcomes.
3. Incorporate spatial structure into prediction by representing counties as nodes in a geographic graph constructed from spatial adjacency relationships, enabling neighboring communities to be modeled as relationally connected rather than independent observations.
4. Assess the impact of graph convolutional learning by implementing a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) to determine whether localized neighborhood aggregation improves predictive performance when modeling disaster assistance outcomes across geographically connected counties.
5. Compare tabular and graph-based deep learning approaches within a unified regression framework to evaluate how model architecture and spatial information influence the ability to model county-level disaster assistance outcomes.

## Data Summary

The county-level 2018 Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) dataset is a relative vulnerability dataset designed to identify counties that may be more vulnerable before, during, and after hazardous events. The dataset integrates socioeconomic indicators derived primarily from the U.S. Census Bureau American Community Survey and converts them into percentile-based vulnerability rankings across U.S. counties. SVI variables are organized through a structured pipeline of derived fields. These include raw estimate fields (`E_`), margin-of-error fields (`M_`), derived percentage fields (`EP_`), percentage margin-of-error fields (`MP_`), percentile-ranked indicator fields (`EPL_`), theme sums (`SPL_`), theme percentile rankings (`RPL_THEME1` through `RPL_THEME4`), and the overall percentile ranking (`RPL_THEMES`). Higher percentile values correspond to greater relative social vulnerability compared with other counties.

In this study, the SVI dataset is used as a feature dataset representing underlying social and demographic conditions at the county level. 

### Data Sources

1. **US County 2018 SVI dataset:** [CDC/ATSDR Download](https://www.atsdr.cdc.gov/place-health/php/svi/svi-data-documentation-download.html)
2. **US County 2018 SVI dataset documentation:** [SVI 2018 Documentation (PDF)](https://svi.cdc.gov/map25/data/docs/SVI2018Documentation_01192022_1.pdf)
3. **US County 2018 TIGER/Line county boundaries:** [Census.gov TIGER/Line](https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/TIGER2018/COUNTY/)
4. **FEMA Individuals and Households Program disaster assistance data API (2018–2020):** [OpenFEMA API Endpoint](https://www.fema.gov/api/open/v2/IndividualsAndHouseholdsProgramValidRegistrations)

The TIGER/Line geographic data provide county boundary geometries used to construct spatial adjacency relationships for the graph-based models developed later in the study.

### Core SVI Feature Set: 15 Social Vulnerability Variables

The primary predictive feature set consists of 15 socioeconomic indicators, organized into four themes within the official SVI framework. These indicators are expressed as percentages (`EP_*` variables) derived from underlying American Community Survey estimates.

**Theme 1: Socioeconomic Status**
This theme captures economic hardship and limited access to financial resources.

* **`E_POV` / `EP_POV`:** persons below poverty level
* **`E_UNEMP` / `EP_UNEMP`:** civilian unemployed population age 16+
* **`E_PCI` / `EP_PCI`:** per capita income
* **`E_NOHSDP` / `EP_NOHSDP`:** persons age 25+ with no high school diploma

**Theme 2: Household Characteristics**
This theme captures age-related dependency, disability, and family structure vulnerabilities.

* **`E_AGE65` / `EP_AGE65`:** persons aged 65 and older
* **`E_AGE17` / `EP_AGE17`:** persons aged 17 and younger
* **`E_DISABL` / `EP_DISABL`:** civilian noninstitutionalized population with a disability
* **`E_SNGPNT` / `EP_SNGPNT`:** single-parent households with children under 18

**Theme 3: Minority Status and Language**
This theme captures minority population representation and language barriers.

* **`E_MINRTY` / `EP_MINRTY`:** minority population percentage, defined as persons who are Hispanic or Latino of any race or non-Hispanic persons belonging to racial minority groups
* **`E_LIMENG` / `EP_LIMENG`:** persons age 5+ who speak English “less than well”

**Theme 4: Housing Type and Transportation**
This theme captures housing structure, crowding, and transportation access.

* **`E_MUNIT` / `EP_MUNIT`:** housing in structures with 10 or more units
* **`E_MOBILE` / `EP_MOBILE`:** mobile homes
* **`E_CROWD` / `EP_CROWD`:** occupied housing units with more people than rooms
* **`E_NOVEH` / `EP_NOVEH`:** households with no vehicle available
* **`E_GROUPQ` / `EP_GROUPQ`:** persons living in group quarters

### FEMA Disaster Assistance Data Summary

Disaster assistance outcomes used in this study are obtained directly from the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) OpenFEMA API, specifically the Individuals and Households Program (IHP) Valid Registrations dataset. This dataset records household-level disaster assistance provided to individuals following federally declared disasters and includes information on financial assistance amounts, geographic location, and disaster identifiers. 

The dataset is accessed programmatically using the OpenFEMA API endpoint. The following fields are retrieved directly from the OpenFEMA API:

* **`disasterNumber`:** FEMA disaster identifier
* **`incidentBeginDate`:** date when the disaster incident began
* **`fips`:** county-level FIPS geographic identifier
* **`ihpAmount`:** total FEMA Individuals and Households Program assistance awarded for a registration

Because the FEMA dataset records assistance at the individual registration level, the data are aggregated to the county level using the FIPS geographic identifier. The final modeling target is constructed by summing all `ihpAmount` values within each county across the 2018–2020 period. This produces a county-level measure of total disaster assistance received during 2018-2022, which serves as the regression target for the study's ML models. The FEMA dataset also includes county-level FIPS codes, allowing the aggregated assistance outcomes to be merged directly with the SVI dataset and county geometries. This shared geographic identifier enables the integration of socioeconomic vulnerability indicators, disaster assistance outcomes, and spatial adjacency relationships within a unified county-level dataset used for modeling.

## Model Design & Training Procedure

The primary predictor variables used in this study are the 15 `EP_*` variables from the CDC/ATSDR SVI 2018 dataset. These variables represent estimated percentages of populations. Using the `EP_*` variables as the main features provides percentage-based variables as normalized and interpretable representations of population characteristics, allowing counties of different sizes to be compared on a consistent scale.

The MLP baseline serves as a tabular deep learning model that learns nonlinear relationships among county-level features while treating each county as an independent observation. This provides a strong baseline for evaluating how well socioeconomic indicators alone explain variation in disaster assistance outcomes. 

In contrast, the graph-based model incorporates spatial structure by representing counties as nodes in a geographic graph constructed using spatial adjacency relationships. Edges in this graph connect neighboring counties based on Queen contiguity, allowing information to propagate between geographically adjacent regions during training. The graph model follows the Graph Neural Network (GNN) framework, where node representations are updated through message passing that aggregates feature information from neighboring counties. In this study, the message-passing mechanism is implemented using a Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) architecture. The GCN layer performs graph convolution operations that aggregate normalized neighborhood features, enabling localized spatial smoothing and structured feature propagation across the county network.

All models are trained as regression models to predict continuous county-level disaster assistance outcomes. Prior to training, feature variables are log-transformed and standardized based on insights from exploratory data analysis to address skewness and scale differences across variables. Training is conducted using supervised learning with an MSE loss function, and models are optimized using stochastic gradient–based methods. The dataset is divided into training, validation, and test subsets. The training set is used to fit model parameters, while the validation set is used for hyperparameter tuning, early stopping, and model selection through a series of ablations. After the final model configuration is selected, performance is evaluated on the held-out test set to obtain an unbiased estimate of predictive accuracy and generalization. Model performance is evaluated using RMSE and $R^2$. This evaluation framework enables consistent comparison between the tabular MLP baseline and the spatial graph-based GCN model.
